file_name: Sousa et al. - Decision Support Software for Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection (DECIME).json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning classifiers, specifically Single Layer Perceptron (SLP), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), and Support Vector Machine (SVM), combined with feature extractors (Central Moments, GLCM, LBP) to detect and classify melanoma skin cancer from dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel decision support software (DECIME) for melanoma detection, describes the methodology including data acquisition from the HAM10k dataset (17,805 samples), feature extraction (GLCM, LBP, Central Moments), and machine learning classification (SLP, MLP, SVM). It presents original experimental results, including accuracy metrics (93% accuracy with Central Moments and MLP) and deployment on a Raspberry Pi, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Decision Support Software for Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection (DECIME)
journal: Advances in Artificial Intelligence for Healthcare
brief_summary: This study proposes DECIME, a decision support software for melanoma skin cancer detection using computer vision and artificial intelligence. The system utilizes the HAM10k dataset, comprising 17,805 dermoscopic images, and extracts features via Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM), Local Binary Pattern (LBP), and Central Moments. Classification is performed using Single Layer Perceptron (SLP), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithms. The best-performing model achieved 93% accuracy using Central Moments with an MLP classifier, demonstrating that the problem is nearly linearly separable. Finally, the software was deployed on a Raspberry Pi 3B+ with a webcam to enable portable, real-time lesion analysis for healthcare professionals.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The dataset (HAM10k) contained 8,903 samples of melanoma. In the experiment, 7,122 samples were used for training/testing and 1,781 for the test set.
The negative class for the binary classification task. The dataset contained 8,902 samples of non-melanoma. In the experiment, 7,122 samples were used for training/testing and 1,780 for the test set.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into 'melanoma' and 'non-melanoma' classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper describes a binary classification task using the HAM10k dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using classifiers such as Single Layer Perceptron (SLP), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), and Support Vector Machine (SVM) combined with feature extractors (GLCM, LBP, Central Moments). Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics for these two classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10k (Dermoscopic Pigmented Skin Lesions)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions, made available by Alexander Scarlat via Kaggle. It contains images of melanoma and non-melanoma skin cancers.

use.training: 7,122 samples (randomly selected from each class) were used to compose the training set. K-Fold cross-validation (K=10) was applied to this set for validation.
use.validation: Used via 10-Fold cross-validation on the training subset.
use.testing: The remaining samples (1,781 melanoma and 1,780 non-melanoma) were used as an independent test set unknown to the classifier during training.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used for validation of the training data to estimate parameters and evaluate classifier performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reported accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics using K-Fold cross-validation and an independent test set, but did not perform statistical significance testing between models.

HAM10k.notes: The best overall result was 93% accuracy using Central Moments with an MLP classifier. The paper notes that SLP and MLP produced similar results, suggesting the problem is nearly linearly separable. The Central Moments features used were mu11, mu21, mu12, mu30, and mu03. Results are based on 10-Fold cross-validation for training/validation and an independent test set of 1,781 melanoma and 1,780 non-melanoma samples.

architecture_used.main_model: Single Layer Perceptron (SLP), Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), and Support Vector Machine (SVM)

conclusions: The proposed DECIME software successfully detects melanoma skin cancer with high accuracy (93%) using the Central Moments feature extractor combined with a Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) classifier on the HAM10k dataset.

The problem of detecting melanoma using Central Moments features is nearly linearly separable, as evidenced by the similar performance between the Single Layer Perceptron (SLP) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) classifiers.

Less robust machine learning techniques (SLP, MLP, SVM) combined with specific attribute extractors can achieve results comparable to or superior to more complex models while requiring significantly lower computational cost and memory resources.
Deploying the decision support software on a Raspberry Pi 3B+ with a webcam creates a simple, fast, and portable solution suitable for healthcare professionals.
The portable nature of the deployed software improves accessibility for patients in regions with difficult access to care units or those unable to reach care facilities, enabling home care support.
The system is designed as a decision support tool, meaning generated results must be analyzed by a doctor for proper clinical conclusions.
important_remarks: The study relies on the HAM10k dataset, which is known to have class imbalance and potential selection bias; the paper does not report performance on external, independent datasets, limiting generalizability.

The problem of melanoma detection using Central Moments features was found to be 'nearly linearly separable,' as evidenced by similar performance between Single Layer Perceptron (SLP) and Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), suggesting that complex deep learning architectures may be unnecessary for this specific feature set.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to compare model performances; conclusions about superiority are based solely on point estimates of accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

The software is explicitly defined as a decision support tool, not a diagnostic device; results must be analyzed by a doctor for proper clinical conclusions, highlighting its role as an adjunct rather than an autonomous diagnostic system.

Deployment on a Raspberry Pi 3B+ demonstrates low computational cost and portability, but the study does not report real-world clinical validation, user acceptance, or performance in uncontrolled clinical environments.

The dataset split was random (7,122 for training/validation, 3,561 for testing) rather than patient-wise or site-wise, which risks data leakage if multiple images from the same patient or site appear in both sets, though the paper does not explicitly confirm patient independence.

The study uses traditional machine learning classifiers (SLP, MLP, SVM) with hand-crafted features (GLCM, LBP, Central Moments) rather than deep learning, which may limit sensitivity to subtle visual patterns compared to state-of-the-art CNNs, despite the reported 93% accuracy.
